major_topics: Non-transferred plasma arc and plasma jet burners
Hafnium electrodes for compressed-air plasma cutting
Bob Gage and the serendipitous discovery of plasma cutting
Pfender's four characteristic lengths in atmospheric plasma
Paschen breakdown and field emission as arc ignition theories
Ragnar Holm and electrical contact theory
Touch start and high-frequency arc ignition
Maecker plasma jet theory and the J×B force
Inverter power supplies and pulse-current welding
Variable polarity plasma arc for aluminum
Arc blow and electromagnetic forces in welding
Titanium submarines, SSN-21 Seawolf, and the DARPA composite submarine workshop
Heat flow in welding: Fourier, Rosenthal, Christensen, Chai lineage
cases_present: NASA space shuttle main tanks (variable polarity plasma)
Bob Gage / Union Carbide Speedway Labs plasma discovery
Ragnar Holm and Westinghouse electrical contacts
Swedish cadmium ban in light switches
SSN-21 Seawolf design budget and DARPA workshop
Liberty Ships and submerged arc / Union Melt
Daniel Rosenthal traveling heat source equation (MIT)
Nils Christensen dimensionless heat flow (MIT, Trondheim)
Nunsen Chai distributed heat source PDP-11/23 simulations
named_persons: Arthur Amos Noyes (unnamed, 'guy who founded Caltech')
notes: Lecture covers plasma arc physics, arc ignition mechanisms, electromagnetic effects in arcs, and the historical development of heat-flow theory in welding. Tom takes a five-minute break around 38:00. The titanium submarine / DARPA composite workshop digression (p82-p89) is a substantial historical aside Tom returns to from earlier lectures in the series. Lecture ends with a preview of Friday's heat-flow lecture.
